items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-9-1b, no person may operate an aircraft within Class B airspace unless the aircraft is equipped with an operable two-way radio capable of communicating with ATC and an operable radar beacon _____ with automatic altitude reporting capability.
items.explanation: Per 7-9-1b, Class B operations require both an operable two-way radio AND an operable radar beacon transponder with automatic altitude reporting capability.

choices.text: transponder

items.question_text: Under 7-9-2a, what must VFR aircraft obtain to operate in Class B airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-2a, VFR aircraft must obtain an ATC clearance to operate in Class B airspace. This differs from Class C where only two-way radio communication is required, and TRSA where participation is voluntary.

choices.text: A filed VFR flight plan.
An ATC clearance.
A discrete transponder code assignment.
Two-way radio communication only.

items.question_text: Under 7-9-4b, which VFR aircraft must be separated from aircraft weighing more than 19,000 pounds and turbojets in Class B?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-4b, all VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing more than 19,000 pounds and turbojets. This creates a size-based separation requirement unique to Class B.

choices.text: Only IFR aircraft need separation from heavy and turbojet aircraft.
Only VFR aircraft weighing less than 12,500 pounds must be separated from larger aircraft.
All VFR aircraft must be separated from aircraft weighing more than 19,000 pounds and turbojets.
No additional separation is required beyond standard wake turbulence procedures.

items.question_text: Under 7-9-6, what is the separation requirement for VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft in Class B?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-6, VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. Traffic advisories and safety alerts must still be provided.

choices.text: They need not be separated from any aircraft type in Class B.
They need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft.
They must be separated by the same standards as fixed-wing aircraft.
They need not be separated only if operating below 500 feet AGL.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-9-8, the _____ must specify the approach interval for Class B operations.
items.explanation: Per 7-9-8, as with TRSA (7-7-6), the tower must specify the approach interval. This is consistently a tower function across TRSA and Class B.

choices.text: tower

items.question_text: Under 7-9-3a, how should large turbine engine-powered airplanes be cleared to/from the primary airport in Class B?

choices.text: Only via published SIDs and STARs, regardless of traffic conditions.
Using altitudes and routes that avoid VFR corridors and airspace below Class B, to the extent practical.
On a direct route at any altitude requested by the pilot.
Below Class B airspace to keep them clear of VFR traffic.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-3a, to the extent practical, large turbine engine-powered airplanes should be cleared using altitudes and routes that avoid VFR corridors and airspace below Class B. This minimizes conflicts between high-performance aircraft and VFR traffic.

items.situation: Two VFR aircraft are operating in Class B airspace on converging courses. Both are small general aviation aircraft under 19,000 pounds.
items.para_context: Per 7-9-5a, provide mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between all aircraft in Class B. Per 7-9-4b, VFR aircraft must be separated from aircraft more than 19,000 pounds and turbojets. Per 7-9-5b, apply merging target procedures per 5-1-4.
items.question_text: What services must be provided to these two VFR aircraft?

choices.text: Only traffic advisories; safety alerts are optional in Class B for VFR aircraft.
Radar separation, because all aircraft in Class B must be separated from each other.
Mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts, and apply merging target procedures if targets appear likely to merge.
No additional services because small VFR aircraft need not be separated from each other and advisories are workload-permitting.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-5, traffic advisories and safety alerts are MANDATORY between all aircraft in Class B (not workload-permitting). Separation from aircraft over 19,000 lbs/turbojets (7-9-4b) does not apply here, but advisory and alert services do.
